name: United Kingdom
summary: English-native with the world's largest, deepest British-Bangladeshi community (~652,000, centred on London's Tower Hamlets/Banglatown), a top global AI hub, dual citizenship retained both ways (UK is explicitly on Bangladesh's DNC list), a ~6-year citizenship path at native-English ease, NHS access via the health surcharge and world-class universities — but weighed down by very high tuition (£32k–45k for an AI MSc) and London costs, the Graduate Route being cut to 18 months (2027), a Reform-UK-driven anti-immigration/anti-Islam turn, and a 2025 White Paper proposing to double the settlement (ILR) wait to 10 years. Derived overall is comfortably above the 60% inclusion line.

links.title: GOV.UK — visas and immigration (official)
GOV.UK — citizenship & living in the UK (official)

job-market.summary: London is a top global AI/tech hub with strong, English-native demand and high salaries, though entry-level hiring has tightened.

pros.text: London is a top global AI hub (≈80% of UK AI-skills demand; ~200% YoY growth in AI roles), with software engineers earning ~£73k–142k and AI engineers ~£64k–112k, in an English-native market.
Deep multi-city market (London, Manchester, Cambridge, Edinburgh) and a clear Skilled Worker sponsorship route.

cons.text: Entry-level hiring has tightened (AI tooling plus rigorous selection), and the immigration-restriction climate cools sponsorship appetite.

links.title: Levels.fyi — Software Engineer salary, London
IT Brief — UK developer pay & AI demand (2025)

visa-access.summary: A deep, well-trodden Bangladeshi student pipeline via the UK High Commission/VFS in Dhaka, though tuition, maintenance funds, the IHS and a TB test raise the cash bar.

pros.text: A very well-established Bangladeshi student pipeline via the UK High Commission and VFS in Dhaka, with efficient (often ~3-week priority) processing and high approval for genuine students.

cons.text: The cash bar is high — full tuition plus maintenance funds, the IHS (£1,035/yr) and a UKVI-approved TB test — and the overall migration climate is tightening.

links.title: GOV.UK — Student visa (official)
AIMS — UK student visa guide for Bangladeshi students (2026)

citizenship.summary: Dual citizenship retained both ways, a ~6-year path with native-English ease (only the Life in the UK test), and a strong passport — but a 2025 White Paper proposes doubling the ILR wait to 10 years.

pros.text: The UK allows dual citizenship and is explicitly on Bangladesh's DNC list, so a naturalising Bangladeshi keeps BD nationality; for English natives the only real hurdle is the Life in the UK test.
Citizenship currently follows ~5 years to ILR plus 12 months; the British passport ranks ~6th on Henley 2026 (~186).

cons.text: The May-2025 Immigration White Paper proposes raising the ILR qualifying period from 5 to 10 years ('earned settlement'), which would push citizenship to ~11 years — a major, unresolved policy risk.

links.title: House of Commons Library — changes after the 2025 immigration White Paper
GOV.UK — apply for citizenship (naturalisation, official)

post-study-work.summary: The Graduate Route gives master's graduates 2 years of unrestricted work (cut to 18 months from 2027), converting to a Skilled Worker visa; each spouse qualifies.

pros.text: The Graduate Route gives master's graduates 2 years (PhDs 3) of fully unrestricted work — any job, self-employment, business — with a switch to Skilled Worker; each graduating spouse qualifies.

cons.text: From January 2027 the Graduate Route is cut from 2 years to 18 months for bachelor's/master's graduates, and Skilled Worker salary thresholds have risen.

links.title: GOV.UK — Graduate visa (official)
House of Commons Library — Graduate Route reduction (2027)

spouse-family.summary: Strong spouse work rights on the Graduate/Skilled Worker routes and free state schooling — but master's-student dependants have been barred since 2024, so the study phase needs both spouses on their own student visas.

pros.text: Dependants of Graduate and Skilled Worker visa holders get unrestricted work rights, children attend state school free, and if both spouses study each holds their own visa and Graduate Route.

cons.text: Since January 2024 taught-master's students cannot bring dependants (only PhD/research students can), so during the study phase the couple must each hold a student visa rather than one accompanying the other.

links.title: GOV.UK — your partner and children (dependants, official)

msc-study.summary: World-class English-native CS/AI (Imperial, UCL, Oxbridge, Edinburgh) in a fast 1-year format, but international tuition is very high (£32k–45k for an AI MSc).

pros.text: World-class English-native CS/AI master's (Imperial, UCL, Oxford, Cambridge, Edinburgh) in an efficient 1-year format that qualifies for the Graduate Route; students may work 20 hrs/week in term.

cons.text: International tuition is very high (£32,000–45,000 for an AI MSc), on top of the IHS and London living costs.

links.title: Imperial College London — MSc Artificial Intelligence (official)

pr-pathway.summary: ILR via the Skilled Worker route at 5 years with only B1 English (native) — but the White Paper's proposed 10-year ILR and raised salary thresholds add real risk.

pros.text: ILR via Skilled Worker is currently reachable at 5 years with only B1 English (native) and the Life in the UK test, and ICT salaries clear the threshold.

cons.text: The 2025 White Paper proposes doubling the ILR wait to 10 years (earned settlement), and Skilled Worker salary thresholds (£38,700) and conditions have been raised — a significant predictability hit.

links.title: Faegre Drinker — 'Earned Settlement' reshaping UK settlement

income-cost.summary: High salaries are eroded by very high tuition, the IHS and London housing, leaving a modest surplus in the early phase.

pros.text: High London tech salaries support a reasonable surplus and remittance once in Skilled Worker employment.

cons.text: The study phase is very expensive (£32k–45k tuition plus IHS and London rent), and London housing keeps upfront and ongoing costs high.

links.title: Eduvisors — UK visa & IHS costs for Bangladeshi students

healthcare.summary: Full NHS access from arrival via the Immigration Health Surcharge (£1,035/yr), though NHS waiting times are long.

pros.text: Paying the IHS (£1,035/yr) gives students and workers full NHS access on the same basis as residents from arrival, free at the point of use.

cons.text: NHS waiting times for GPs, specialists and elective care are long, and childcare is expensive.

links.title: GOV.UK — Immigration Health Surcharge (official)

culture-language.summary: English-native with no language barrier for work, study or status, and the deepest South-Asian/Bangladeshi cultural fabric in the West — though Reform-UK-driven anti-migrant sentiment is rising.

pros.text: English is the native language — no language friction for work, PR or citizenship — and the UK has the deepest established Bangladeshi/South-Asian community and cultural fabric in the West.

cons.text: Anti-immigration and anti-Muslim sentiment is rising with the surge of Reform UK, denting social acceptance despite the large diaspora.

links.title: British Bangladeshis (overview)

safety-law.summary: Solid rule of law (WJP 14th) and a strong Equality Act, but with urban crime concerns and rising Islamophobia.

pros.text: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 14th of 143, with a strong, enforced anti-discrimination framework (Equality Act 2010).

cons.text: London has notable urban (incl. knife) crime, and Islamophobic incidents have risen amid the Reform UK surge.

links.title: World Justice Project — United Kingdom ranks 14th (2025 profile)

politics.summary: A stable democracy, but immigration policy is tightening sharply (White Paper) and Reform UK's surge is pushing an anti-immigration, anti-Islam agenda.

pros.text: Institutions are stable, and skilled migration (Skilled Worker, Global Talent) remains operative with a clear framework.

cons.text: Immigration policy is tightening fast (10-year ILR proposal, Graduate Route cut, higher thresholds), and Reform UK's surge (leading several 2025–26 polls) is pushing anti-immigration and anti-Islam policy (e.g. proposing to ban church-to-mosque conversions).

links.title: Hyphen — what Reform UK stands for (UK Muslims)

tax.summary: A moderate-high burden (income tax to 45% + NI), with the non-dom regime abolished in 2025 (a narrow new-arrival FIG regime remains) and a UK–Bangladesh treaty.

pros.text: Individual taxation with a tax-free personal allowance, a narrow 4-year FIG relief for new arrivals, and a UK–Bangladesh double-taxation treaty supporting repatriation.

cons.text: Income tax reaches 45% plus National Insurance, and the long-standing non-dom regime was abolished in 2025.

links.title: GOV.UK — income tax rates (official)

muslim-diaspora.summary: The strongest diaspora fit in the set: the world's largest British-Bangladeshi community (~652,000), abundant mosques and halal everywhere, and a rich South-Asian ecosystem — tempered by the Reform-UK climate.

pros.text: The UK hosts the world's largest British-Bangladeshi community (~652,000; Tower Hamlets/Banglatown, Newham, Luton, Birmingham, Oldham) within a ~6.5% Muslim population, with abundant mosques (East London Mosque) and halal everywhere, and the richest South-Asian ecosystem in the West (Brick Lane, Southall).

cons.text: Day-to-day sentiment is strained by Reform UK's rise and rising Islamophobia, even though legal accommodation (Equality Act) is strong.

links.title: British Bangladeshis (overview)
Islam in the United Kingdom (overview)
